title: The Expiring Key
passage:
For nearly a century the hotel room turned on a brass key, and the brass key had one incurable flaw: a guest who copied it, or who simply neglected to hand it back, could let himself in again months later. Re-cutting every lock after every mislaid key was ruinously expensive, so most hotels quietly gambled that few departed guests would ever return. The engineer Halvard Sunde, who patented an early keycard lock in 1976, did not set out to save anyone a walk to the front desk. His aim was narrower than that. He wanted the guest's credential to expire: a punched card, and later a magnetic one, carried a code the lock could be told to forget the instant the guest checked out, so that the next arrival's card opened a door the previous card no longer would. The smooth swipe and the tidy wallet came afterward, and largely by accident.
— Dagny Holt, design history

questions.prompt: According to the text, what was Sunde's main aim in designing the keycard lock?

options.text: To spare guests the nuisance of a heavy brass key by letting them open the door with a quick swipe instead.
To make each guest's card stop working once they left, so a copied or unreturned credential could no longer open the room.
To cut hotels' costs by replacing cheap punched and magnetic cards more easily than expensively re-cutting brass locks.
To let the front desk keep a record of exactly which rooms each guest had entered during their stay.

questions.rationale: TYPE-001 direct detail (paraphrase of the sentences naming Sunde's aim). The text states 'His aim was narrower than that. He wanted the guest's credential to expire', then explains that the lock could 'forget' a card's code at checkout 'so that the next arrival's card opened a door the previous card no longer would'; B paraphrases exactly that. A = contradiction/scope_error: the swipe and 'tidy wallet' are named as things that 'came afterward, and largely by accident', and the text explicitly says Sunde 'did not set out to save anyone a walk to the front desk'. C = outside_knowledge/wrong_location: the passage says re-cutting locks was 'ruinously expensive' as the brass key's flaw, but never claims cards were cheaper or that cost-cutting was the aim — it names credential expiry as the aim instead. D = outside_knowledge: an entry-logging capability the passage never mentions.

generator_meta.topic: why hotels replaced mechanical brass keys with keycards — the driver was making a guest's credential expire at checkout, not convenience
generator_meta.spelling_note: AmE held throughout ('afterward' not 'afterwards'; 'neighbor'/'-ise' forms not triggered; no BrE-only spellings present).
generator_meta.originality_note: Halvard Sunde and Dagny Holt are invented; the 1976 date and the keycard-lock premise are generic technology-history colour, not a quotation of any real patent, person, or publication. No famous-thesis anchoring — the tested point rides on the passage's own account of the aim.
